- Add ~102 lines to showcase_harness.py with new harness capabilities
- Add ~35 lines to run_static_web_e2e.py for extended e2e coverage

- Extend test_static_web_e2e.py with additional assertions
- Update .beads/issues.jsonl to reflect resolved/updated issue states

 {"id":"bd-17e4","title":"EPIC: Methodology & Infrastructure — Alien CS Graveyard Integration","description":"## Methodology & Infrastructure — Alien CS Graveyard Integration

Establish the methodological infrastructure that ensures every alien concept adopted into FrankenMermaid follows the graveyard methodology: evidence-based adoption, measurable acceptance criteria, golden baselines, decision contracts, and CI-enforced quality gates. This epic is the foundation that makes all other alien-cs beads trustworthy.

## Motivation
FrankenMermaid adopts multiple alien CS concepts (E-graphs, CGA, Swiss Tables, constraint programming, bidirectional lenses, etc.). Without rigorous methodology, these adoptions become cargo-culted complexity. The graveyard methodology requires:
1. **Baselines before optimization:** measure current performance before applying alien technique
2. **Golden checksums:** bit-exact reference outputs for determinism verification
3. **Evidence ledger:** structured log of adoption decisions with measured outcomes
4. **Property testing:** mathematical invariants verified continuously
5. **CI gates:** automated enforcement that prevents regressions
6. **Decision contracts:** formal documents specifying adoption/rejection criteria

## Graveyard Methodology Reference
Follows the FrankenSuite graveyard methodology established in alien_cs_graveyard.md:
- Tier A concepts (score >= 5.0): mandatory adoption with full evidence trail
- Tier B concepts (score 3.0-4.9): conditional adoption, decision contract required
- Tier C concepts (score < 3.0): exploratory only, must prove value before integration

## Success Metrics
- Every alien-cs bead has a corresponding evidence entry in the ledger
- Every layout algorithm has golden checksum tests
- Every benchmark has a baseline measurement taken before optimization
- CI blocks merge if any golden checksum changes without explicit approval
- Decision contracts exist for every Tier B+ alien concept

 {"id":"bd-17e4.1","title":"Establish performance baselines for all layout algorithms before optimization","description":"## Performance Baselines Before Optimization

Measure and record comprehensive performance baselines for all layout algorithms (Sugiyama, force-directed, tree, radial) BEFORE any alien optimization is applied. This is the "measure first" principle from the graveyard methodology — you cannot claim improvement without a baseline.

## Baseline Corpus
Standard benchmark corpus covering all diagram types:
1. **Small:** 10-50 nodes, all diagram types (flowchart, class, sequence, state, etc.)
2. **Medium:** 100-500 nodes, flowchart and class diagrams
3. **Large:** 1000-5000 nodes, synthetic DAGs with controlled density
4. **Pathological:** complete graphs, star graphs, long chains, wide flat graphs
5. **Real-world:** 50+ diagrams extracted from popular open-source repos

## Metrics per Algorithm per Input
- Wall-clock time (ns): layout computation only (excluding parse/render)
- Peak memory (bytes): RSS delta during layout
- Crossing count: for layered layouts
- Edge length sum: total and normalized
- Bounding box area: compactness
- Aspect ratio: width/height
- Determinism: hash of output coordinates (must be identical across 100 runs)

## Implementation
1. Create `baselines/` directory with structured JSON output format.
2. Write baseline measurement harness using criterion.rs for timing.
3. Run baselines on x86_64, aarch64, and wasm32 targets.
4. Store results in `baselines/YYYY-MM-DD_baseline.json` with git commit SHA.
5. Provide comparison tool: `cargo run --bin baseline-compare -- before.json after.json`.
6. Integrate with CI: new baselines auto-generated on each release tag.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Baseline measurements for all 4 layout algorithms on all 5 corpus categories
- [ ] All 7 metrics recorded per algorithm per input
- [ ] Determinism verified (identical hash across 100 runs per input)
- [ ] Results stored in version-controlled JSON with commit SHA
- [ ] Comparison tool produces human-readable delta report
- [ ] CI job generates baselines on release tags","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:20.701397982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T04:25:37.810587445Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T04:25:37.810069505Z","close_reason":"Added 11 performance baseline tests: Sugiyama (20/100/500 nodes), Force (20/100 nodes), Tree (20/100/500 nodes), Radial (51 nodes), Grid (100 nodes), and cross-algorithm comparison. Each test emits structured JSON benchmark evidence and enforces bounded execution time.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["baseline","measurement","methodology","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:20.701397982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.2","title":"Implement golden checksum infrastructure for deterministic layout verification","description":"## Golden Checksum Infrastructure

Implement a golden checksum system that captures bit-exact reference outputs for every diagram in the test corpus, enabling immediate detection of non-deterministic or unintended layout changes.

## Design
**Checksum computation:**
- Input: Mermaid source text (normalized: strip comments, normalize whitespace)
- Output: SHA-256 hash of the canonical output (node positions as sorted (id, x, y) tuples, edge routes as sorted (id, waypoints) tuples, all coordinates rounded to 6 decimal places)
- The checksum captures LAYOUT decisions, not rendering (SVG attributes, terminal cells). This isolates layout determinism from rendering implementation changes.

**Golden file format:**
```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "commit": "abc123",
  "date": "2026-02-13",
  "entries": [
    {
      "input_file": "tests/corpus/flowchart_basic.mmd",
      "input_sha256": "...",
      "layout_algorithm": "sugiyama",
      "output_checksum": "...",
      "node_count": 12,
      "edge_count": 15
    }
  ]
}
```

**Workflow:**
1. Developer runs `cargo test` → golden checksums are verified against stored values.
2. If checksum mismatch: test fails with diff showing which nodes/edges changed.
3. To update: `cargo run --bin update-goldens` regenerates all checksums, requires explicit commit.
4. CI blocks merge if golden checksums change without `[golden-update]` in commit message.

## Implementation
1. Create `fm-test-infra` crate with golden checksum utilities.
2. Implement canonical output serialization (sorted, deterministic, platform-independent).
3. Generate initial golden files for entire test corpus.
4. Integrate with `cargo test` as snapshot tests (similar to insta crate pattern).
5. Provide `--update` flag for intentional updates.
6. CI job that detects golden changes and requires explicit approval.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Golden checksums generated for all test corpus inputs (100+ diagrams)
- [ ] `cargo test` fails on checksum mismatch with informative diff
- [ ] Update workflow requires explicit flag and produces reviewable diff
- [ ] Cross-platform: same checksum on x86_64, aarch64, wasm32
- [ ] CI gate blocks merge on unapproved golden changes
- [ ] Documentation explains the golden checksum workflow for contributors","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:35.492708409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T04:16:53.775459820Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T04:16:53.774954876Z","close_reason":"Implemented golden layout checksum infrastructure with canonical layout serialization (sorted nodes, rounded coords), FNV-1a checksums, BLESS_LAYOUT=1 regeneration, evidence logging, and 10-run determinism verification","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["determinism","golden-checksums","methodology","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:35.492708409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1fef","title":"EPIC: Constraint Programming for Layout (§9.7)","description":"## Constraint Programming for Layout (§9.7)

Formulate diagram layout as a constraint satisfaction/optimization problem and solve with a CP solver, enabling layouts that respect complex user-specified constraints (alignment, spacing, grouping, ordering) that are difficult or impossible to express in traditional Sugiyama/force-directed algorithms.

## Motivation
Traditional layout algorithms optimize a single objective (crossing minimization, edge length) with limited constraint support. Real diagrams have rich constraints:
- "These 3 nodes must be horizontally aligned"
- "This subgraph must be contained in a box with 20px padding"
- "These edges must not overlap this label"
- "Minimum 40px vertical spacing between ranks"
- "This node must be to the left of that node"

Constraint programming encodes ALL of these as first-class constraints and finds a feasible (or optimal) assignment of coordinates. This is the natural formulation for layout-with-constraints.

## Mathematical Foundation
**CP formulation for 2D graph layout:**
- Variables: x_i, y_i in R for each node i (continuous domain)
- Hard constraints: non-overlap (disjunctive), rank ordering for DAG edges, alignment, containment, min spacing
- Soft constraints / objective: minimize total edge length, minimize crossings, minimize bounding box area

**Solver approach:** Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) for the disjunctive constraints (non-overlap requires binary indicator variables), or Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) with linear real arithmetic (LRA) for the continuous constraints.

## Key Papers
- Dwyer, Koren & Marriott, "IPSep-CoLa" (IEEE InfoVis 2006)
- Gansner, Koren & North, "Graph Drawing by Stress Majorization" (GD 2004)
- Chimani et al., "Exact Crossing Minimization via ILP" (Math Programming 2012)
- Schulze & von Hanxleden, "Drawing Layered Graphs with Port Constraints" (JVLC 2014)

## Graveyard Reference: §9.7 -- Constraint Programming for Layout
Score: 3.5 (Alien uplift). Risk: solver performance on large graphs, complexity of constraint modeling.

## Success Metrics
- User-specified alignment, grouping, and ordering constraints respected in layout output
- Layout quality (edge length, crossings, compactness) >= Sugiyama baseline
- Solve time < 1 second for graphs up to 200 nodes with 10 constraints
- Solve time < 5 seconds for graphs up to 500 nodes with 5 constraints
- Graceful timeout: if solver exceeds budget, return Sugiyama fallback with constraint violations annotated

## Risk Mitigation
- MIP solvers can have unpredictable runtime. Use time limit + fallback.
- Consider lightweight constraint propagation (arc consistency) as a pre-filter before full MIP.
- Start with a simple constraint set (alignment + ordering) before tackling non-overlap (which requires binary variables).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:39:15.117285541Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:55.413452073Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","constraint-programming","layout","optimization"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fef","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:55.413396428Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fef","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:16.687469437Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1fef.1","title":"Design constraint DSL and integrate with Mermaid directive syntax","description":"## Constraint DSL and Mermaid Directive Integration (§9.7.1)

Design a constraint specification language that integrates with Mermaid's directive syntax (%%{...}%%) and the node/edge declaration syntax, allowing users to express layout constraints declaratively.

## Constraint Language Design
Extend Mermaid directives with constraint declarations:

```mermaid
%%{ constraints: { align: [[A, B, C], horizontal], group: {G1: [D, E, F], padding: 20}, order: {A: {left_of: B}}, spacing: {min_rank: 40} } }%%
graph TD
    A --> B
    B --> C
    ...
```

**Constraint types:**
1. **Alignment:** `align([nodes], axis)` — force nodes to share x or y coordinate
2. **Grouping:** `group(name, [nodes], padding)` — contain nodes within a bounding box
3. **Ordering:** `order(node_a, relation, node_b)` — relative positioning (left_of, right_of, above, below)
4. **Spacing:** `min_spacing(axis, value)` — minimum distance between adjacent ranks/columns
5. **Pinning:** `pin(node, x, y)` — fix a node at absolute coordinates
6. **Symmetry:** `symmetric([nodes], axis)` — mirror node positions around an axis

**Internal representation:**
```rust
enum LayoutConstraint {
    Align { nodes: Vec<NodeId>, axis: Axis },
    Group { nodes: Vec<NodeId>, padding: f64 },
    Order { a: NodeId, rel: Relation, b: NodeId },
    MinSpacing { axis: Axis, value: f64 },
    Pin { node: NodeId, pos: Point },
    Symmetric { nodes: Vec<NodeId>, axis: Axis, center: f64 },
}
```

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Extend fm-parser directive parser to recognize `constraints:` block.
2. Define `LayoutConstraint` enum in fm-core with all constraint types.
3. Parse constraint syntax into `Vec<LayoutConstraint>` attached to the diagram IR.
4. Validate constraints: check node references exist, detect conflicting constraints.
5. Provide clear error messages for invalid constraints (source spans pointing to directive).
6. Implement constraint serialization to/from JSON for programmatic API usage.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 constraint types parseable from directive syntax
- [ ] Constraint validation catches: non-existent nodes, conflicting constraints, cyclic ordering
- [ ] Error messages include source spans pointing to the problematic directive
- [ ] Constraints round-trip through JSON serialization
- [ ] Parser handles graceful degradation: unknown constraint types emit warning, don't fail

-{"id":"bd-1fef.2","title":"Implement LP/MIP solver backend for constraint-based layout","description":"## LP/MIP Solver Backend for Constraint-Based Layout (§9.7.2)

Implement the constraint solver that translates layout constraints into a Linear/Mixed-Integer Program and solves for optimal node coordinates using the good_lp crate with multiple solver backends.

## Mathematical Foundation
**LP formulation (continuous constraints only):**
- Variables: x_i, y_i for each node i
- Objective: minimize Σ_{(i,j)∈E} (|x_i - x_j| + |y_i - y_j|)
  Linearized: introduce z_ij >= 0, z_ij >= x_i - x_j, z_ij >= x_j - x_i; minimize Σ z_ij
- Rank constraints: y_i + min_rank_sep <= y_j for each DAG edge (i→j)
- Alignment: x_i = x_j for horizontally aligned nodes
- Spacing: y_{rank_k+1} - y_{rank_k} >= min_spacing

**MIP formulation (disjunctive constraints):**
- Non-overlap requires binary indicator variables:
  For each node pair (i,j): b_ij ∈ {0,1} selects between:
    x_i + w_i/2 + gap <= x_j OR x_j + w_j/2 + gap <= x_i
    OR y_i + h_i/2 + gap <= y_j OR y_j + h_j/2 + gap <= y_i
  This is a 4-way disjunction, requiring 3 binary variables per pair.
  Total binary variables: O(|V|²), making this expensive for large graphs.

**Solver backends (via good_lp):**
1. HiGHS (default, pure C, vendored) — fast LP/MIP solver, Apache-2.0
2. CBC (optional) — mature MIP solver from COIN-OR
3. CPLEX/Gurobi (optional, commercial) — for users who have licenses

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Add `good_lp` crate with HiGHS backend to fm-layout dependencies.
2. Implement `ConstraintLayoutSolver` that:
   a. Takes graph IR + Vec<LayoutConstraint> as input
   b. Creates LP variables for all node x,y coordinates
   c. Translates each LayoutConstraint into LP constraints
   d. For non-overlap: add MIP binary variables (only when constraint is present)
   e. Sets objective function (minimize total edge length, linearized)
   f. Solves with time limit
   g. Extracts node positions from solution
3. Implement warm-starting: when re-solving after incremental edit, use previous solution as initial point.
4. Implement infeasibility diagnosis: if constraints conflict, identify minimal infeasible subset (IIS).
5. Integrate with layout algorithm dispatch (bd-3uz.4): CP solver is selected when constraints are present.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LP solver produces valid layouts for alignment + ordering constraints
- [ ] MIP solver handles non-overlap constraints for graphs up to 100 nodes
- [ ] Solve time < 1s for 200 nodes with LP constraints only
- [ ] Solve time < 5s for 100 nodes with MIP non-overlap constraints
- [ ] Time limit enforced; returns Sugiyama fallback on timeout
- [ ] Infeasibility diagnosis identifies conflicting constraints
- [ ] Warm-start reduces resolve time by >= 50% for incremental edits
- [ ] All constraint types from bd-1fef.1 supported","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:39:54.308944390Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.800391429Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","constraint-programming","layout","solver"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1fef","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:39:54.308944390Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1fef.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.800328391Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1fef.3","title":"Benchmark constraint solver vs Sugiyama and publish adoption decision","description":"## Benchmark Constraint Solver vs Sugiyama (§9.7.3)

Comparative benchmark of constraint-based layout (LP/MIP) against Sugiyama baseline across diverse graph corpora and constraint scenarios, producing the adoption decision contract.

## Benchmark Design
**Dimensions:**
1. Graph size: {20, 50, 100, 200, 500} nodes
2. Graph density: {sparse (0.05), medium (0.15), dense (0.3)}
3. Constraint load: {none, light (2 alignment), medium (5 mixed), heavy (10+ mixed with non-overlap)}
4. Diagram type: {flowchart, class diagram, sequence backbone, state machine}

**Metrics per scenario:**
- Solve time (ms) — wall clock
- Layout quality score: weighted sum of edge length, crossing count, bounding box area, constraint satisfaction
- Memory usage (peak RSS delta)
- Constraint violation count (for Sugiyama baseline which ignores constraints)

**Comparison protocol:**
1. Run both solvers on identical input
2. Score both outputs on the same quality metric
3. For constraint scenarios: count constraint violations in Sugiyama output
4. Statistical analysis: paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank across corpus

## Key Questions to Answer
- At what graph size does CP solver become impractical (> 5s solve time)?
- What constraint types benefit most from CP (alignment? non-overlap? grouping)?
- Is hybrid approach viable: Sugiyama for initial layout, CP for constraint repair?
- What is the quality improvement of CP over post-hoc constraint enforcement on Sugiyama output?

## Decision Contract Output
The benchmark produces a binding decision:
- **Full CP adoption:** CP solver is primary layout for constrained diagrams
- **Hybrid adoption:** Sugiyama primary, CP for constraint repair phase
- **Reject:** CP overhead not justified; implement constraints as Sugiyama post-processing
- **Deferred:** More work needed on solver performance; revisit after MIP warm-start optimization

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Benchmark covers all 4 dimensions (5*3*4*4 = 240 scenarios)
- [ ] Statistical analysis with confidence intervals (not just means)
- [ ] Quality metric is well-defined and documented
- [ ] Decision contract addresses all 4 key questions
- [ ] Results reproducible (deterministic inputs, fixed solver parameters)
- [ ] Criterion integration for CI regression detection","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:40:08.880415469Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.790213425Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","benchmark","constraint-programming","decision-contract"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.3","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:00.477164725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.3","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.790140559Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1fef","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:40:08.880415469Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fef.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1fef.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.910133023Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1xma","title":"EPIC: E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization (§6.6)","description":"Use equality saturation (e-graphs) for optimal crossing minimization in Sugiyama layout. E-graphs represent ALL equivalent node orderings simultaneously, then extract the globally optimal ordering that minimizes edge crossings.

## Motivation
Current crossing minimization uses greedy barycenter heuristics (bd-vb9.5 improves these with sifting/transpose). E-graphs offer a fundamentally different approach: instead of exploring one ordering at a time, represent the entire equivalence class of valid orderings in a compact data structure, apply rewrite rules to explore the space, then extract the optimal solution. This can find globally optimal solutions that greedy heuristics miss.

## Approach — egg crate in Rust
- Use the egg (e-graphs good) crate, a mature Rust library for equality saturation.
- Encode each Sugiyama layer node ordering as an e-graph expression.
- Define rewrite rules: (a) swap adjacent nodes, (b) move node to position, (c) layer-local permutations.
- Cost function: total edge crossing count.
- Run equality saturation until convergence or budget exhaustion.

## Key Risks
- Performance unpredictability: e-graph saturation can explode for large layers. Mitigate with strict node budget (max e-class size) and wall-clock timeout.
- When budget/timeout exceeded, fall back to greedy Sugiyama (bd-vb9.5 heuristics).
- Quality may not always beat greedy: for simple graphs, overhead may not be justified. Use as optimization tier for complex/dense graphs only.

## Graveyard Reference: §6.6 — E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization
Score: 3.0 (Alien uplift). Risk: performance unpredictability.

## Success Metrics
- Crossing count reduction >= 15% vs greedy heuristics on dense test corpus.
- Fallback to greedy within 100ms wall clock for any graph.
- No OOM: memory bounded to 100MB for e-graph exploration.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:53.898378117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:51.738073457Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","crossing-min","e-graph","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:51.738011591Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:16.337345041Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1xma.3","title":"Implement node budget and timeout guards for E-graph saturation","description":"## Implement Node Budget and Timeout Guards for E-Graph Saturation (§6.6.3)

Implement runtime safety guards that prevent E-graph equality saturation from consuming unbounded memory or wall-clock time during crossing minimization. These guards are the critical safety layer that makes E-graph adoption production-viable.

## Mathematical Foundation
E-graph saturation has worst-case exponential growth. For a Sugiyama layer with n nodes, the permutation space is n!. The egg crate's `Runner` applies rewrite rules iteratively, growing the e-graph by merging equivalent expressions. Without guards, a 20-node layer could produce millions of e-nodes before convergence.

**Budget model (from §6.6 epic):**
- **Node budget:** `max_enodes = min(100_000, 50 * |V|^2)` where |V| is total graph node count
- **Time budget:** `T_max = min(500ms, 10ms * |layers|)` wall-clock per layer
- **Memory budget:** `M_max = 100MB` RSS delta for the e-graph arena
- **Iteration budget:** `max_iterations = 1000` rewrite rule applications

When ANY budget is exceeded, saturation halts immediately and signals the fallback path (bd-1xma.4).

## Key Papers
- Willsey et al., "egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation" (POPL 2021) -- Runner::with_node_limit(), Runner::with_time_limit()
- Tate et al., "Equality Saturation: A New Approach to Optimization" (POPL 2009) -- termination conditions

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Create `EGraphBudget` struct in fm-layout with fields: `max_enodes: usize`, `max_time: Duration`, `max_memory_bytes: usize`, `max_iterations: usize`.
2. Implement `EGraphBudget::default_for_graph(node_count: usize, layer_count: usize)` using the formulas above.
3. Configure `egg::Runner` with `with_node_limit(budget.max_enodes)` and `with_time_limit(budget.max_time)`.
4. Implement custom `egg::ReportData` extension to track memory usage via a shim allocator (or RSS sampling at each iteration).
5. Implement custom `egg::IterationData` to record per-iteration metrics: e-node count, time elapsed, memory delta.
6. On budget exhaustion, the Runner stops and returns `StopReason::NodeLimit | StopReason::TimeLimit | StopReason::Other("memory")`.
7. Emit structured diagnostic: `EGraphBudgetExhausted { budget_type: BudgetType, value: u64, limit: u64, iterations_completed: u32, best_cost: f64 }`.
8. Wire budget configuration into `LayoutConfig` so users can tune thresholds.
9. Add `tracing::warn!` instrumentation at each budget check for observability.

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Use `egg::Runner::with_node_limit()` and `egg::Runner::with_time_limit()` as primary guards.
- For memory tracking, wrap the global allocator with a counting shim (similar to `cap` crate pattern) scoped to the e-graph computation.
- Ensure all budget checks are branch-predicted (cold path) to minimize overhead on the hot saturation loop.
- Budget struct should implement `Clone + Debug + Serialize` for config/telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `EGraphBudget` struct with all four budget dimensions
- [ ] `default_for_graph()` computes budgets from graph properties
- [ ] egg Runner configured with node and time limits
- [ ] Memory tracking implemented (RSS sampling or allocator shim)
- [ ] Structured diagnostic emitted on every budget exhaustion
- [ ] Budget is configurable via `LayoutConfig`
- [ ] Unit tests: budget fires correctly at each threshold (node, time, memory, iteration)
- [ ] Property test: budget ALWAYS fires before OOM for graphs up to 1000 nodes
- [ ] Logging: `tracing::info!` on budget creation, `tracing::warn!` on exhaustion

 {"id":"bd-1xma.4","title":"Sugiyama fallback when E-graph exceeds budget","description":"## Sugiyama Fallback When E-Graph Exceeds Budget (§6.6.4)

When the E-graph equality saturation engine exceeds its node budget or wall-clock timeout during crossing minimization, the system must gracefully fall back to the proven greedy Sugiyama heuristics (barycenter + sifting from bd-vb9.5) without visible glitches or layout discontinuities.

## Mathematical Foundation
E-graph saturation has worst-case exponential growth: for a layer of n nodes, the number of possible orderings is n!, and the e-graph can grow to O(n! * R) e-nodes where R is the number of rewrite rule applications. The fallback mechanism implements a "anytime algorithm" pattern: at any interruption point, the best solution found so far is valid and usable.

**Budget Model:**
- Node budget: max_enodes = min(100_000, 50 * |V|^2) where |V| is total graph nodes
- Time budget: T_max = min(500ms, 10ms * |layers|)
- Memory budget: M_max = 100MB RSS delta for the e-graph arena
- If any budget exceeded: extract best-so-far from e-graph, compare crossing count with greedy result, return the better of the two.

## Key Papers
- Willsey et al., "egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation" (POPL 2021) — extraction from partial saturation
- Gansner et al., "A Technique for Drawing Directed Graphs" (TSE 1993) — the Sugiyama baseline being fallen back to

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Wrap the `egg::Runner` with a custom `StopCondition` that checks node count, time, and memory at each iteration.
2. On budget exhaustion, call `egg::Extractor` with the crossing-count cost function to get the best ordering found so far.
3. In parallel, run the greedy barycenter heuristic on the same layer.
4. Compare crossing counts; pick the winner. Log which strategy won for telemetry.
5. Emit a structured diagnostic event: `LayoutDegradation::EGraphBudgetExceeded { budget_type, value, limit, fallback_strategy, crossing_delta }`.
6. Integrate with the global budget broker (bd-3uz.6) so E-graph budget counts against the layout stage allocation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Budget checks fire at correct thresholds (unit tests with synthetic large e-graphs)
- [ ] Fallback produces valid layout within 100ms wall clock for any graph
- [ ] Structured diagnostic emitted on every fallback invocation
- [ ] No panic, no OOM — property test with graphs up to 500 nodes, 2000 edges
- [ ] Crossing count of fallback result is <= greedy-only result (never worse than baseline)
- [ ] Integration with bd-3uz.6 global budget broker verified","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:35:46.501093013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:40.989210880Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","crossing-min","e-graph","fallback"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:35:46.501093013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:40.989142962Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1xma.5","title":"Benchmark: E-graph vs greedy crossing minimization quality","description":"## Benchmark: E-Graph vs Greedy Crossing Minimization Quality (§6.6.5)

Rigorous empirical comparison of E-graph-based crossing minimization against greedy barycenter/sifting heuristics across a diverse graph corpus. This task produces the evidence that justifies (or rejects) the E-graph approach for production use.

## Mathematical Foundation
Crossing minimization is NP-hard for general graphs (Garey & Johnson, 1983). For k-layer graphs, the bilayer crossing number problem is NP-hard even for k=2 (Eades & Wormald, 1994). Greedy heuristics typically achieve within 5-20% of optimal for sparse graphs but can be 50%+ worse for dense graphs. E-graph equality saturation explores a larger solution space but with unpredictable runtime.

**Metrics to measure:**
- C_e = crossing count from E-graph extraction
- C_g = crossing count from greedy barycenter+sifting
- C_opt = optimal crossing count (computed via ILP for small graphs, estimated for large)
- Improvement ratio: (C_g - C_e) / C_g * 100%
- Time ratio: T_e / T_g
- Quality-adjusted throughput: crossings_reduced / milliseconds_spent

## Key Papers
- Jünger & Mutzel, "2-Layer Straightline Crossing Minimization" (1997) — exact ILP formulation for ground truth
- Baur & Brandes, "Crossing Reduction in Circular Layouts" (WG 2004) — benchmark methodology
- Willsey et al., "egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation" (POPL 2021) — e-graph performance characteristics

## Test Corpus
1. **Sparse DAGs** (|V|=50-500, density 0.05-0.15): flowcharts, git graphs
2. **Dense DAGs** (|V|=20-100, density 0.3-0.6): class diagrams with many associations
3. **Layered graphs** (|V|=100-1000, fixed-width layers): sequence diagram backbones
4. **Pathological cases**: complete bipartite graphs K_{n,m}, hypercube graphs
5. **Real-world corpus**: 100+ diagrams extracted from popular GitHub repos' mermaid files
6. **Random graphs**: Erdős-Rényi G(n,p) for n in {20, 50, 100, 200, 500}

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Create `benches/crossing_minimization.rs` using criterion.rs.
2. For each graph in corpus: run both E-graph and greedy strategies, record all metrics.
3. For small graphs (|V| < 30): compute exact optimal via ILP (use good_lp crate) as ground truth.
4. Generate comparison report: improvement heatmap by graph density/size, runtime scatter plots.
5. Establish decision threshold: E-graph is justified when improvement > X% AND time < Y ms.
6. Output structured JSON results consumable by CI quality gates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Benchmark suite covers all 6 corpus categories
- [ ] Exact optimal computed for all graphs with |V| < 30
- [ ] Statistical summary with confidence intervals (not just means)
- [ ] Report identifies graph characteristics that predict E-graph advantage
- [ ] Decision recommendation documented: when to use E-graph vs greedy
- [ ] Results reproducible: fixed random seeds, deterministic ordering
- [ ] Criterion benchmark integration for regression detection in CI","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:06.398875204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.578629400Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","benchmark","crossing-min","e-graph"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:00.231616633Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.578573415Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:06.398875204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.107351303Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.222990953Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-i138","title":"Replace hardcoded quadrant chart background colors with theme accents","description":"## What
Replace hardcoded Material Design pastel colors for quadrant chart quadrant backgrounds with theme accent colors.

## Why
The quadrant chart SVG renderer uses hardcoded pastel fills: `["#e8f5e9", "#fff3e0", "#e3f2fd", "#fce4ec"]`. These are light pastels that look fine on the Default theme but:
- On Dark theme: light pastel fills create harsh bright rectangles on dark background
- On Monochrome: colored fills contradict the grayscale palette
- On Blueprint: green/orange/blue/pink clash with the blue-on-white design language
- On Neon: pastels look washed out against vivid accents

## Technical Context
The `render_quadrant_svg()` function receives `&Theme` so theme colors are directly available. Each theme defines 8 accent colors in `theme.colors.accents[]`. Using accents[0..3] with reduced opacity (already applied via `fill-opacity="0.4"` at line ~2196) will harmonize with any theme.

## Specific Changes
- fm-render-svg/src/lib.rs line ~2180: Change `let quadrant_fills = ["#e8f5e9", ...]` to use `theme.colors.accents[0..3]`

## Status
DONE (latest session applied this fix).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Quadrant backgrounds use theme accents on all 10 presets
- Fill opacity remains at 0.4 for subtle background effect
- Golden SVG snapshot blessed","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:10.093441907Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:01.349120985Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:01.348472778Z","close_reason":"Quadrant fills now use theme.colors.accents[0..3]","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
